text: Jacob looked up and saw Esau coming with four hundred men, so he arranged his children among Leah, Rachel, and the two maids.
He put the maids and their children first, Leah and hers next, and Rachel and Joseph last.
Jacob himself went ahead of them, bowing to the ground seven times as he approached his brother.
Esau ran to meet him, embraced him, fell on his neck, and kissed him, and both wept.
Esau looked up and saw the women and children, asking who they were; Jacob said they were his, given graciously by God.
The maids and their children came near and bowed down.
Then Leah and her children bowed, and finally Joseph and Rachel bowed.
Esau asked about the herds he had met on the way; Jacob said they were to find favor in his sight.
Esau said he had enough already and Jacob should keep what was his.
Jacob insisted, saying if he had found favor, Esau should accept the gift, since seeing his face was like seeing God's face, and he had received Jacob kindly.
Jacob urged him to accept the gift God had graciously given him, and Esau accepted after Jacob insisted.
Esau suggested they set out together, with him going ahead.
Jacob said his flocks and children were weak, and if driven hard even one day, the whole flock would die.
He asked Esau to go ahead while he moved slowly at the pace of his livestock and children, meeting him later in Seir.
Esau offered to leave some of his men with him; Jacob said there was no need, he only wanted to find favor.
So Esau returned that day on his way to Seir.
Jacob instead journeyed to Succoth, built a house for himself, and made shelters for his livestock, naming the place Succoth.
Jacob arrived safely at the city of Shechem in Canaan and camped near the city.
He bought the plot of land where he pitched his tent from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father.
There he set up an altar and called it El-Elohe-Israel.
